mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C‑Class C350 / C400 Petrol engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned M272 V6 typically costs £3,500–£5,500 supply only, with the M276 twin‑turbo higher at £4,500–£7,500. Fitting adds £1,200–£1,800. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your C‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C350 / C400 Petrol engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which generation your car is. The W204's M272 (2007–2014) is a characterful naturally aspirated V6, but early cars (pre‑2008) have a notorious balance shaft weakness. The W205's M276 twin‑turbo (2015–2021) has earned a strong reliability reputation but is sensitive to maintenance neglect — timing chain tensioner rattle and oil leaks are the main watchpoints. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the C350 / C400 Petrol engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: M272 (W204): balance shaft gear wear (pre‑2008 engines), timing chain issues, and intake manifold problems. M276 (W205): timing chain tensioner rattle (cold start), oil leaks (valve covers, turbo lines, camshaft sensors), and turbocharger wear. Both engines demand regular maintenance.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a C350 / C400 Petrol engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. A W204 C350 is a £4,000–£10,000 vehicle — a £4,000–£4,500 recon is 40–113% of the car's value. For lower‑value examples (sub‑£5,000), replacement may exceed our 70% Mercedes scrap threshold. A W205 C400 is a £10,000–£20,000+ vehicle — a £5,000–£6,000 recon is 25–60% of the car's value; replacement is viable in most cases. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C350 / C400 Petrol engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The C350 covers M272 (W204, 2007–2014) and the C400 covers M276 (W205, 2015–2021). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a C350 / C400 Petrol?

acceptedAnswer.text: On M272 (W204): check the engine number — if it's within the affected range (up to 2729...30468992), budget for balance shaft replacement. On M276 (W205): regular oil changes with 5W‑40 oil on the 'severe schedule' (5,000 miles) are essential. Have the timing chain tensioners and check valves inspected at the first sign of cold‑start rattle. Address oil leaks promptly to prevent wiring harness damage. At the first sign of rattle, vibration or oil leaks, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes C‑Class C350 / C400 Petrol Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across both generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C350 / C400 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). V6 engines require more disassembly than four‑cylinder units.

problems.title: Balance Shaft Gear Wear (M272 — W204, Pre‑2008)
problems.symptoms: Rattling or grinding noise from the front of the engine, rough idle, increased engine vibration, engine warning light (P0016/P0017 codes), metal debris in the oil.
problems.replacementCost: £3,500–£5,500 (full engine replacement if damage is extensive)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattling or grinding, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Early M272 engines (up to engine number 2729...30468992) have a balance shaft gear made from soft metal that wears prematurely. If this gear fails, metal debris can circulate through the engine, causing catastrophic damage. Check the engine number before buying a W204 C350 — if it falls within the affected range, budget for balance shaft replacement or walk away. A replacement balance shaft kit with an updated timing gear is available.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Rattle (M276 — W205 C400)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (2–3 seconds), rattling or clattering from the front of the engine, poor timing performance, misfires.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The M276's timing chain tensioners are a known weak point — they can lose oil pressure overnight, causing a brief rattle on cold start. This can progress to timing chain stretch and camshaft adjuster wear. Mercedes issued a technical service bulletin (LI05.10‑P‑056435) recommending the installation of check valves and updated chain tensioners. Regular oil changes with 5W‑40 oil on the "severe schedule" (5,000 miles) are essential. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the tensioners and check valves inspected.

problems.title: Oil Leaks — Valve Covers, Turbo Lines & Camshaft Sensors (M276 — W205 C400)
problems.symptoms: Oil spots under the vehicle, burning oil smell, oil residue on the engine, oil leaking into the wiring harness.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (valve cover gaskets, turbo oil lines, camshaft sensor seals)
problems.recommendation: Oil leaks are a prevalent issue on the M276 engine. Common sources include valve cover gaskets, turbocharger oil lines, and camshaft position sensors that can leak oil into the wiring harness. If you notice oil spots or a burning smell, have the engine inspected. Replacing gaskets and seals promptly prevents more serious damage and expensive wiring harness repairs.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C350 / C400 Petrol — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before metal debris causes engine failure
Install check valves and updated tensioners — permanent fix
Replace or rebuild — cheaper than collateral damage

rows.problem: Severe engine damage (balance shaft failure / chain jump)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C350 / C400 Petrol? Check These First
buyIf: * M272 (W204): post‑2008 engine (outside affected engine number range) or balance shaft already replaced
* M276 (W205): no cold‑start rattle — timing chain tensioners in good condition
* No oil leaks — valve covers, turbo lines and camshaft sensors dry
* Full Mercedes service history with regular oil changes
avoidIf: * Pre‑2008 M272 with no balance shaft history — walk away
* Cold‑start rattle on M276 with no tensioner history — timing chain issues likely
* Oil leaks with no diagnosis — potentially expensive
* Poor service history — both engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C350 and C400 petrol V6s are the performance heart of the C‑Class range — the W204's M272 is a characterful naturally aspirated V6, but early cars have a notorious balance shaft weakness. The W205's M276 twin‑turbo is more modern and responsive, but timing chain tensioner rattle and oil leaks are the main watchpoints. Buy on full service history, check the engine number on M272 cars, and never ignore a cold‑start rattle.

cta.label: → Compare C350 / C400 Petrol engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes C‑Class C350 / C400 Petrol (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Engine Number (M272) — Check if within affected balance shaft range (up to 2729...30468992)
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling or grinding; balance shaft (M272) or timing chain tensioner (M276) indicator
* Oil Leaks — Check under the car and around the engine; valve covers, turbo lines and camshaft sensors
* Service History — including which generation (W204 or W205) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Turbo Health (M276) — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo failure indicator
* Engine Vibration — Check for rough idle or increased vibration; balance shaft wear indicator (M272)
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
